title: The feast-and-famine money cycle explained
subtitle: Why irregular income trains the nervous system to brace, and how the cycle maintains itself.
excerpt: A short reading on the feast-and-famine money pattern — the boom-and-bust cycle where money arrives in a rush and drains out before the next wave. Why it develops, how the nervous system responds to irregular income, and why tapping and visualisation target the body rather than the spreadsheet. Useful context before working with the feast-or-famine practices.
sourceNotes: The nervous system response to income irregularity is documented across stress physiology research and somatic therapy literature. The feast-or-famine money pattern is addressed in Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "End the feast/famine cycle."

practice.whenToUse: Read before working with the feast-or-famine tapping, energy statement, or visualisation. Also useful if the cycle is familiar but it isn't clear why it keeps returning despite better budgeting.

content.text: The feast-and-famine money cycle is a pattern most visible in freelance, self-employed, and commission-based income — but it isn't exclusive to those situations. It appears wherever income is unpredictable: a good month followed by a slow one, a bonus followed by a wait, a project payment that drains out before the next arrives. The cycle is partly practical (irregular income is irregular) and partly nervous system (the body has learned to expect the swing).

When income arrives unreliably often enough, the nervous system adapts. It stops treating money as a stable resource and starts treating it as a scarce one — present sometimes, absent other times, unpredictable by default. The body's response to scarcity is preparation: hoard, spend quickly before it disappears, brace for the shortage.

This adaptation makes sense as a survival response. If income has historically been unreliable, the nervous system is correct to treat it as unreliable. The problem is that the adaptation tends to persist even when the income stabilises. The body continues to brace for the famine even in months where the income is steady, because the pattern of bracing is now the default, not the exception.

The cycle has a self-reinforcing quality. In the flush period, when money arrives, the relief of the payment landing combines with the anxiety of knowing it won't last. The impulse is to spend it before it disappears, because the body knows from experience that it will. The spending depletes the flush period faster than it needs to, which confirms the pattern: money arrives and goes.

In the gap period, when money hasn't arrived yet, the bracing that was learned in previous gap periods returns, even if the bank balance is still positive. The body is already in scarcity mode before the scarcity is real. The heightened alert makes the gap period feel longer and harder than the numbers require.
Why tapping and embodiment work on this pattern

The feast-and-famine cycle is not only a budgeting problem — it is a nervous system state. Better financial planning helps the practical side: a buffer account, a regular payment structure, saving in flush months. But planning doesn't automatically retrain the body's response. The body may continue to brace even after the practical cycle is broken.

Tapping, visualisation, and embodiment practices target the nervous system response directly. Tapping on the fear in the gap period lowers the body's alert. Visualisation of a level pool (rather than a churning sea) installs a counter-image for the body to reference. The practices don't change the income — they change the body's relationship to income, which changes the decisions made with it.

The tapping practice addresses the anxiety of both the flush period and the gap. The energy statement offers a release of the bracing and an allow for steady flow. The visualisation installs the level-pool image. The embodiment practice gives the body the shape of receiving steadily. Use them in the gap period when the anxiety is loudest, or in the flush period when the bracing has arrived too early.

The nervous system response to income irregularity is documented across stress physiology research and somatic therapy literature. The feast-or-famine money pattern as a mindset work focus is adapted from Day 4 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), which addresses the cycle's nervous system roots.
